headline: Investment Analysis: 19 Linden St, Brookline Village
subheadline: Two viable development paths — grounded in by-law research and real-market data

recommendation.summary: Both the 14-unit density strategy and the 5-6 unit luxury strategy produce strong returns at a $5.5M acquisition. The deal works — but the pro forma must account for Brookline's inclusionary zoning requirement (Section 4.08), which neither original model captured. Key regulatory questions remain open; resolve before committing capital.

ratings.note: Irreplaceable: Green Line access, top schools, Longwood Medical proximity, scarcity of large lots
New construction at $950-$1,400/sf; 29% YoY appreciation; deep institutional buyer pool
Both scenarios produce 13-25%+ margins at $5.5M. Workable.
Inclusionary zoning (15% affordable), setback formula, and parking requirements are now quantified — all manageable but reduce upside vs. naive models
Adaptive reuse avoids 18-month demo delay; both scenarios keep existing house; Building Inspector contact established
Keeping the house = no demo delay, community goodwill, potential Historic Tax Credits, and possible Section 5.21 public benefit bonus
Transit Overlay reduces parking to 1 space/unit; MBTA Communities Act may unlock future upzoning

zoningAndByLaws.subtitle: Critical regulatory factors that shape every scenario

densityFormula.rule: 3,000 sqft lot area for first unit + 1,000 sqft for each additional
densityFormula.source: Brookline Zoning By-Law Table 5.01; confirmed by Amr's analysis

criticalSections.summary: 15% of units must be Affordable at 80% AMI (for-sale) or 50% AMI (rental). Triggers at 4+ units. Payment-in-lieu alternative exists.
criticalSections.impactOnAmrPlan: 14 units x 15% = 2.1 -> 2 affordable units. Revenue reduction ~$2.2M (from $23.0M to $20.8M)
criticalSections.impactOnBeatkaPlan: 5 units x 15% = 0.75 -> 1 affordable unit or payment-in-lieu. Revenue reduction ~$500-800K
criticalSections.openQuestion: Confirm rounding rules (2 vs 3 units?) and current payment-in-lieu amount with Planning Dept.
criticalSections.source: Brookline Zoning By-Law Section 4.08; Brookline Housing Division

criticalSections.summary: Side setback = (Building Length / 10) + 10 feet. Longer buildings require wider side yards.
criticalSections.openQuestion: Need lot survey (width x depth) to determine maximum building width after setbacks

criticalSections.summary: Standard: 1.4-2.0 spaces/unit. Transit Overlay District (within 0.5mi of Green Line): 1.0 space per 1BR+ unit.

transitOverlay.note: 19 Linden is ~5 min walk from Brookline Village Green Line D station — almost certainly qualifies

criticalSections.openQuestion: Confirm Transit Overlay boundary includes this parcel. Verify if parking is embedded in Amr's $7M building cost.
criticalSections.source: Brookline Zoning By-Law Section 6.02; 2021 Transit Overlay amendment

criticalSections.summary: Applies to National Register properties. But if the existing house is preserved (both scenarios), the delay is likely avoided entirely. Reach out to preservation department to confirm.
criticalSections.source: Brookline Demolition Delay By-Law Article 5.3; Emily Noel

criticalSections.summary: Allows exceptions to FAR and height limits in exchange for public benefits (e.g., historic preservation, affordable housing beyond minimum). Could unlock additional sellable sqft above the 17,698 FAR cap.
criticalSections.openQuestion: Discuss with Ted Murray whether preservation of the Greek Revival qualifies

criticalSections.summary: 30 ft minimum rear yard for M-1.0 multifamily. Combined with front setback (10-20 ft), lose 40-50 ft of lot depth.

developmentScenarios.name: Scenario A: Maximize Density (Amr's Plan)
developmentScenarios.concept: Preserve existing Greek Revival house as 2 units (3,000 + 1,200 sqft). Build 12 new ~1,000 sqft units behind/adjacent. Underground garage with elevator access to each unit.
developmentScenarios.source: Amr's financial model + density formula analysis

existing.openQuestion: How is the house split into 2 units? Carriage house, basement, or attic apartment? Confirm feasibility and whether it triggers demo delay.

unitMix.farNote: 17,860 sqft is 162 sqft over the FAR 1.0 cap of 17,698. Must trim ~162 sqft from unit sizes or common space.

expenses.parkingNote: Underground garage for ~14 spaces ($1.05-1.4M) — verify if included in building cost

investorReturns.label: Amr's Leveraged Investor Model (pre-IZ adjustment)
investorReturns.note: This is a LEVERAGED return to equity investors. The 23.61% annual return reflects ~3.7x leverage on ~$3.9M equity. Unlevered project return is 12.06%. Leverage amplifies both returns and risk — if revenue falls, equity absorbs first loss.
investorReturns.izAdjustmentNote: These investor returns do NOT yet account for Section 4.08. Adjusted net cash to investors would be ~$1.5-2M lower.

sensitivityAnalysis.title: Sensitivity: New Unit $/sqft vs. Profit (with IZ adjustment, 10 market-rate new units)
sensitivityAnalysis.interpretation: The deal works at $1,200/sqft or higher, even with inclusionary zoning. At $1,500/sqft (Amr's target), margin is 25.4%. The $1,200 floor is supported by comps (45 Bartlett at $1,191/sqft).

acquisitionSensitivity.title: Sensitivity: Purchase Price vs. Profit (at $1,500/sqft, with IZ adjustment)
acquisitionSensitivity.interpretation: Even at the full $5.95M asking price, Amr's plan generates ~$4.8M profit if $1,500/sqft holds. The deal is less price-sensitive than the v1 model suggested — the small-unit premium strategy changes the calculus.

pros: Small units command higher $/sqft — validated by comps
Underground garage with elevator adds luxury feel
cons: More complex construction and sales process
14 parking spaces vs 5 — larger underground garage
FAR is at absolute maximum — no margin for error
Open question on feasibility of splitting existing house into 2 units

developmentScenarios.name: Scenario B: Luxury Large Units (Beatka's Plan)
developmentScenarios.concept: Preserve existing house as a single-family attached home. Add a townhouse and 3-4 attached flats. Underground garage with elevator. A 'small responsible development' that keeps the neighbors onside.
developmentScenarios.source: Beatka Zakrzewski (agent) — sold 40 Kent and 40 Webster Place, one block away, both National Register listed

unitMix.note: Well under FAR 1.0 cap. Only 5 of 15 possible units used — leaves significant density headroom for future value.

expenses.note: Estimated — Beatka's plan lacks a detailed pro forma. Building costs may be lower due to simpler construction (fewer units, less common space, smaller garage).

withInclusionaryZoning.label: Adjusted for Section 4.08 (1 affordable unit or payment-in-lieu)
withInclusionaryZoning.note: 15% of 5 = 0.75 units. Likely 1 affordable unit at ~$400K or a payment-in-lieu fee.
withInclusionaryZoning.alternativePaymentInLieu: If payment-in-lieu is available, total revenue stays ~$20.6M minus the fee. May be more favorable.

withoutIZ.note: Faster timeline due to simpler construction

withIZ_onSite.note: IZ hits harder on large units — one $3.5M unit becoming affordable loses more revenue

withIZ_paymentInLieu.note: If payment-in-lieu is available, margins stay in the 20-25% range. Confirm fee amount with Planning Dept.

sensitivityAnalysis.title: Sensitivity: Blended $/sqft vs. Profit (5 units, no IZ — or with payment-in-lieu)
sensitivityAnalysis.interpretation: Deal works at $1,100/sqft blended. At $1,200 (Beatka's target), margin is 25.7%. Note: large units historically command lower $/sqft than small units.

beatkasCompEvidence.title: Agent's Local Comparable Knowledge
comps: Sold 40 Kent St and 40 Webster Place — one block from 19 Linden, both National Register listed, both adjacent sites
Buyer of those sites is pursuing 70+ units on the 2 sites combined (Green St developments)
Brookline Village pricing trajectory supports $20M+ sell-out in 3-year timeframe
beatkasCompEvidence.agentQuote: I think there's close to a $20M sell out based on today's dollars. It could be higher in 3 years based on the price/sqft trajectory.

pros: Simpler construction and faster timeline (~3.5 vs 4 years)
Only 5 parking spaces needed — smaller, cheaper garage
Fewer IZ units required (1 vs 2), payment-in-lieu may be optimal
Well under FAR cap — no envelope risk
Agent has sold comparable NR-listed properties nearby
Keeping house as single unit avoids any demo delay questions
cons: ~$2-3M less total revenue than Scenario A (before IZ)
Large units sell at lower $/sqft — need $1,100+ blended to work
Does not maximize the lot's development potential (5 of 15 possible units)
If on-site IZ is required (not payment-in-lieu), one $3.5M affordable unit is a big hit
Less detailed pro forma — needs architect/cost estimator to firm up

table.scenarioA: Low (but open question on house split)

combinedSensitivityMatrix.title: Combined Sensitivity: What Breaks the Deal?
combinedSensitivityMatrix.subtitle: Minimum $/sqft needed to achieve target margin, by scenario and purchase price

scenarioA_14units.label: Scenario A (14 units, with IZ)
scenarioA_14units.note: At $5.5M purchase, the deal breaks even at ~$1,060/sqft (well below market) and hits 25% margin at ~$1,500/sqft (Amr's target).

scenarioB_5units.label: Scenario B (5 units, payment-in-lieu assumed)
scenarioB_5units.note: At $5.5M purchase, the deal breaks even at ~$920/sqft blended (well below even median resale) and hits 25% margin at ~$1,250/sqft.

riskMatrix.mitigation: Budget for 15% affordable units in all models. Explore payment-in-lieu as potentially more favorable. Confirm rounding rules.
Get lot survey immediately. The Length/10 + 10 formula may limit building width. Architect must verify 14 units + garage fit within envelope.
Trim 162 sqft from unit sizes or common space. Minor adjustment — does not threaten viability.

riskMatrix.risk: Existing House Split Triggers Demo Delay
riskMatrix.mitigation: Clarify with preservation department whether converting house to 2 units requires exterior changes that trigger 'systematic removal of character-defining elements'. If so, keep as 1 unit (Beatka's approach).

riskMatrix.risk: Underground Parking Cost Not Fully Budgeted
riskMatrix.mitigation: Break out parking as explicit line item. At $75-100K/space, 14 spaces = $1.05-1.4M. Confirm with Amr whether this is within the $7M building cost.
Sensitivity shows deal works at $1,200/sqft (12.9% margin). Pre-sales or broker pricing opinion can de-risk before construction commitment.
Both scenarios keep the existing house — strong community signal. Beatka's 5-unit plan has lowest opposition risk. Early engagement with Preservation Commission.
Historic rehabilitation adds 15-25% cost uncertainty for existing house. New construction behind is more predictable. Get GC estimates early.
Amr's investor model assumes ~$14.4M construction loan. Lock financing terms early. 23.61% investor return is leveraged — rate increases compress equity returns disproportionately.

opportunities.detail: Historic preservation may qualify as a 'public benefit' allowing exceptions to FAR and height limits. Could unlock additional sellable sqft above the 17,698 cap — transformative for Scenario A which is at max FAR.
Reduces parking from 1.4-2.0 to 1.0 space per unit. Saves $350K-700K in garage construction costs for Scenario A. Nearly certain to apply given proximity to Green Line.
Federal HTC: 20% of qualified rehabilitation costs. Massachusetts HTC: additional state credit. Applies to income-producing use of National Register buildings. Both scenarios qualify.

Brookline must zone for 6,990 multifamily units. If this parcel falls in the Section 3A overlay, future density may exceed M-1.0 limits — particularly valuable for Beatka's plan which uses only 5 of 15 current as-of-right units.
29.3% YoY appreciation. Both Beatka and Amr expect pricing to be higher at project completion (3-4 years) than today. Current comps may understate exit pricing.

comparableSales.relevance: Best comp for Scenario A small units — similar size, new construction, 1 block away
Larger unit — supports lower $/sqft for bigger product
Best comp for Scenario B luxury large units — similar size, premium location
Sold by Beatka, 1 block from 19 Linden, both National Register. Buyer pursuing 70+ units on combined sites. Proves NR listing does not block development.
